Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty chaotic this semester. I tried to carve out time in the evenings after work, usually around 9 PM, and I'd put on lecture recordings while making dinner or doing chores. I learn best by listening, so I'd replay audio explanations multiple times. With eight online courses, I had to juggle a lot, and some weeks I managed to study 27 hours, other weeks barely anything. I kept a planner but life kept throwing curveballs at me.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what was due and what was happening in my life. I'd have bursts of intense studying right before deadlines, then quieter periods where I could barely look at course materials. Working with limited resources means I sometimes had to choose between buying groceries or printing materials, which affected when and how I could study. I tried to be consistent but consistency felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford.
Q3: Usually it was panic, honestly. I'd get an email notification about an upcoming deadline or see a discussion post notification, and that would push me to log in. Sometimes I logged in just to check if I'd missed anything important because my anxiety about falling behind was constant. The fear of missing a deadline or an announcement was a strong motivator, even when I was exhausted.
Q4: There was a period mid-semester where I got into a rhythm of checking the LMS every morning with my coffee before my shift. It became almost automatic, like checking my phone. That happened because I'd missed an important announcement once and the panic from that experience conditioned me to check regularly. But that routine fell apart when my work hours changed and my stress levels went through the roof.
Q5: The lecture recordings were absolutely essential for me because I'm an auditory learner. I could download them and listen while commuting or doing housework. The discussion forums were also really helpful because interacting with other students helped concepts click in ways that reading alone never did. I posted quite a bit because hearing other perspectives through written discussions felt like having study partners I couldn't afford to meet in person.
Q6: I often skipped the supplementary reading PDFs because I just didn't have time to sit and read through dense text. My brain doesn't absorb written material as well anyway. I also ignored a lot of the optional practice quizzes early on because I was so focused on just completing the required assignments. Looking back, maybe those quizzes would have helped me prepare better for exams.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could immediately see how it connected to assignments or exams. If I finished something and felt like I actually understood a concept better, that was gold. I also valued anything I could do while multitasking because my time is so limited. Activities that felt like busy work or weren't clearly connected to my grade felt like a waste when I was already stretched so thin.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a trap sometimes. I knew they might help, but with eight courses and limited hours, I had to ruthlessly prioritize. Required activities had clear consequences for not doing them, so they always won. Optional ones made me feel guilty because I knew I should do them but couldn't justify the time. It's like knowing vegetables are good for you but only having money for rice.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded lecture audio files and listened to them constantly when I wasn't near my laptop. I'd take handwritten notes while listening on my phone during bus rides. I also used free YouTube videos to supplement concepts I didn't understand from the course materials. A lot of my studying happened in my head, replaying explanations I'd heard, working through problems mentally while at work.
Q10: So many times. I'd listen to a downloaded lecture three or four times while working my job, but the LMS would only show I accessed it once. I'd have deep discussions about course concepts with a classmate over text messages that helped me understand things better than any forum post. I did a lot of mental rehearsal and self-quizzing that obviously leaves no digital trace.
Q11: All the time. I'd download audio files or save discussion threads and then study from those for days without logging back in. My internet at home isn't always reliable, so offline studying was often my only option. I'd access a video once, take detailed audio notes by recording myself summarizing it, and then review my own recordings repeatedly.
Q12: Around midterms, everything crashed down on me. My work increased my hours, my anxiety spiked, and I felt paralyzed. I'd stare at the LMS login page and feel this wave of dread wash over me. The mindfulness exercises from the intervention helped me breathe through it, but there were weeks where logging in felt like facing a monster. I was scared I'd already failed beyond recovery.
Q13: Constantly. When I was exhausted from work, I'd log in but just scroll without really absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities meant I often had to choose between sleep and studying, and sometimes I chose wrong. Confusion about course material made me avoid certain modules entirely because I didn't have the mental bandwidth to struggle through them. Fatigue made everything feel twice as hard.
Q14: Yes, definitely. Sometimes I'd have the LMS open for hours but I was really just going through the motions. I'd watch videos but realize at the end that I hadn't absorbed a single thing because my mind was elsewhere worrying about bills or work. Those sessions felt like I was performing engagement rather than actually learning. My body was present but my brain had checked out.
Q15: I avoided the exam preparation materials because they terrified me. I knew my exam scores were weak and looking at practice tests made my anxiety spike so badly I'd have to do breathing exercises to calm down. I also postponed complex assignments until the last minute because starting them felt overwhelming. The fear of confirming I didn't understand something was sometimes worse than the deadline pressure.
Q16: Deadlines controlled my life. I'd have these intense bursts of activity right before something was due, sometimes staying up all night. My LMS activity probably looks like a flatline punctuated by explosions. I wish I could have been more consistent, but deadlines were the only thing that could cut through my anxiety and force me to act. Without them, I'd probably have been paralyzed indefinitely.
Q17: Getting good feedback on assignments was like oxygen for my motivation. My assignment scores were actually pretty good, and that feedback kept me going when everything else felt hopeless. But waiting for feedback was torture because my anxiety would spiral imagining the worst. When feedback was delayed, I'd disengage because I didn't know if I was even on the right track.
Q18: The course structure was both helpful and overwhelming. Having clear modules and deadlines gave me something to hold onto, but eight courses meant those structures overlapped in chaotic ways. Weekly patterns helped me plan, but when one course's heavy week coincided with another's, I'd have to make impossible choices. More flexibility would have helped, but I also needed the structure to function.
Q19: The audio and video content made engagement easier because it matched how I learn. What made it harder was when important information was buried in long text documents I didn't have time to read carefully. Also, discussion requirements that demanded multiple posts per week across multiple courses became exhausting. The sheer volume of content across all my courses made deep engagement with any single one really difficult.
Q20: I think that describes me sometimes. High activity might mean someone is trying really hard but struggling to understand, or they're anxious and checking things repeatedly without making progress. It could also mean they're engaging with content but then life gets in the way of actually completing the final submission. Activity and output aren't the same thing, especially when you're fighting just to stay afloat.
Q21: That makes sense to me too. Someone might download everything once and study intensively offline. Or they might be brilliant and need less time with materials to understand them. I wish that described me, but my exam scores show that despite my offline effort, something wasn't clicking. Maybe those students have better study strategies or fewer external stressors eating away at their cognitive resources.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're active and what you click on. But they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't see that I listened to a lecture five times on my phone, or that I was crying from stress while completing an assignment, or that a ten-minute forum post took me two hours because of anxiety. They capture the skeleton of engagement but miss the flesh and blood.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions to check boxes. It means thinking about course concepts when you're not even studying, making connections, wanting to learn more. For me, engagement also means showing up despite being scared, despite anxiety telling me to give up. It's a fight I wage every day, and the LMS only sees the battlefield, not the war.
Q24: The LMS can't show how hard I fought to stay in this course. It can't show the mindfulness breathing I did before every login to manage my anxiety. It can't show that I'm a 24-year-old with limited resources working while taking eight courses, or that every assignment I completed was a victory against circumstances trying to push me out. My resilience doesn't generate data points, but it's the most important part of my story.
